1: The tension between Joshua 11:23 ("Joshua took all the land") and Joshua 13:1 ("a great deal of land remains to be taken") is resolved by recognizing two levels of description: the overall military defeat of organized Canaanite resistance (complete) and the actual settlement of the population (incomplete). This is standard in ANE conquest accounts — Thutmose III's Annals claim all of Canaan while Egyptian records show ongoing Canaanite city-state activity. Joshua is old (zaqen) and "advanced in years" — the appointment of his successor's task is urgent.
2: The Philistines (Pelishtim) are listed as unreduced territory — ironic, since the five Philistine city-states (Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron) would become Israel's greatest military challenge in the period of the judges and early monarchy. The Philistines (likely part of the Sea Peoples migrations of the 12th century BCE) were not yet the dominant coastal power during Joshua's conquest; this list anticipates the situation that developed later. The Geshurites here are not the Transjordanian Geshurites of 13:11 but a Negev-region group.
3: The Shihor east of Egypt is likely the easternmost branch of the Nile delta or the Wadi el-Arish — the traditional southwestern boundary of Canaan. Ekron is the northernmost of the five Philistine cities. The five Philistine lords (sarnei Pelishtim) used the distinctive term seren (a non-Semitic word, possibly related to Greek tyrannos), reflecting the Philistines' non-Semitic origin. Avvim are a pre-Philistine coastal population displaced by the Philistines (Deuteronomy 2:23).
4: The Sidonians controlled the Phoenician coast (modern Lebanon). Aphek at the border of the Amorites in the north is different from the Aphek in the Sharon Plain. The northern coastal and Lebanese territories remained unconquered throughout the period of the judges and were never fully incorporated into Israel's territory. Tyre and Sidon remained independent Phoenician cities that maintained commercial and diplomatic relations with Israel (Solomon's alliance with Hiram, 1 Kgs 5).
5: The Giblites (Gebalites) are the inhabitants of Byblos (Gebal), the ancient Phoenician port city famous for its cedar trade and as the origin of the word "Bible" (via byblos, papyrus/book). Lebanon remained outside Israelite control throughout the entire period. Lebo-hamath (the entrance to Hamath, modern Lebweh on the Orontes) was the traditional northern boundary of the ideal Israelite territory (Numbers 34:8; 1 Kings 8:65).
6: Misrephoth-maim (possibly Tell Mushrefeh near the coast) marked the northeastern extent of Joshua's northern campaign (Joshua 11:8). The divine promise "I will drive out" (first person singular, divine) for the remaining territories distinguishes them from the territories Joshua has already taken. The promise is not retracted — YHWH will complete what Joshua began — but it will require the tribes to continue under divine direction. The book's theology allows for human failure to complete the task without withdrawing the divine promise.
7: The nine and a half tribes east of the Jordan receive their allotments through the lot-casting process (Joshua 14:2). The half-tribe of Manasseh is distributed on both sides of the Jordan: half east (Joshua 13:29-31) and half west (Joshua 17:1-13), reflecting the pragmatic administrative result of their territory request (Num 32) and their large family structure (Numbers 26:28-37). The land division is framed as a divine command, not merely a political arrangement.
8: The Reubenites and Gadites had secured their inheritance east of the Jordan under Moses (Num 32). Their Transjordanian territory (essentially modern Jordan) was outside the boundaries of the Promised Land as defined in Num 34, but within the territory that YHWH had given Israel through military conquest. The text carefully distinguishes this Mosaic grant from the Canaan distribution Joshua oversees.
9: Aroer on the Arnon gorge is a key boundary marker that recurs throughout the Transjordanian boundary descriptions (Deuteronomy 2:36; Joshua 3:12; Joshua 12:2; Joshua 13:16). The Arnon River (Wadi Mujib) cuts dramatically through the plateau into the Dead Sea — a natural geographic boundary between Moab to the south and the Amorite kingdom to the north. Medeba (modern Madaba) was a significant Transjordanian city that appears in the Mesha Stele (the Moabite Stone), an extra-biblical inscription from ca. 840 BCE that confirms and complicates the biblical account of Israelite-Moabite relations.
10: The cities of Sihon's kingdom in the plateau of Heshbon describe a well-established urban network in the Transjordanian highlands. The boundary with Ammon (bnei Ammon) reflects ongoing tensions between Israel and Ammon that continue throughout the judges period (Judg 10-11) and into the monarchy (1 Sam 11; 2 Sam 10). The Ammonite prohibition against entering the assembly (Deuteronomy 23:3) reflects this hostile relationship.
11: Gilead — the forested highland east of the Jordan between the Yarmuk and Arnon rivers — was shared between the tribes and the remaining Geshurite and Maacathite enclaves. The explicit note that these peoples were not expelled (Joshua 13:13) is significant: the conquest narrative does not conceal incomplete obedience. Gilead later becomes the home of Elijah (1 Kings 17:1) and is famous for its healing balm (Jeremiah 8:22).
12: The Rephaim connection for both Og and the Rephaim of Bashan places these pre-Israelite populations in the context of the Bronze Age giant-traditions of the Levant. Bashan's royal cities of Ashtaroth (Tell Ashtara) and Edrei (modern Deraa) controlled the northern Transjordanian plateau. The Deir Alla inscription (8th century BCE) from the Jordan Valley mentions Balaam son of Beor in an Aramaic text — providing extra-biblical confirmation of the Balaam tradition in this region.
13: The failure to drive out the Geshurites and Maacathites "to this day" is a candid editorial note — they remain as enclaves within Israelite-controlled territory throughout the period of writing. Geshur became significant in David's history: he married the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur (2 Samuel 3:3), and his son Absalom fled there after killing Amnon (2 Samuel 13:37-38). The unconquered territory created ongoing complications for later Israelite history.
14: The Levitical non-inheritance is stated here and repeated emphatically in Joshua 13:33. Levi's landlessness was distinctive among ancient peoples — most priestly classes controlled temple estates. The explanation "the offerings made by fire to the LORD God of Israel are their inheritance" (ishei YHWH) means Levi's sustenance comes from the sacrificial system — the portions given to priests from every offering. This theology of divine provision rather than land possession becomes a model for NT thinking about Christian community and support of ministry (1 Corinthians 9:13-14).
15: The allotments to Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh occupy much of the chapter and are given in the same formulaic structure: "Moses gave an inheritance to... according to their clans." The formulaic repetition (Joshua 13:15, 24, 29) emphasizes that this distribution has the same divine authority as the Cisjordanian distribution Joshua oversees. The Transjordanian tribes are full covenant members, not second-class Israelites with a lesser inheritance.
16: The plateau (mishor) of Medeba in Reuben's territory was a flat tableland suitable for agriculture and herding. Aroer marks the southern boundary; the Arnon gorge defined the physical border with Moab. The Mesha Stele (ca. 840 BCE) mentions that Reuben occupied Medeba and Aroer, then Moab recovered them — providing external evidence that this region was genuinely contested between Israelite and Moabite populations throughout the Iron Age.
17: Dibon (Dhiban) is where the Mesha Stele was discovered in 1868. Bamoth-baal (Heights of Baal) was a high place where Balak took Balaam to curse Israel (Numbers 22:41) — the conquest territory includes the very sites of attempted opposition to Israel's advance. Beth-baal-meon (Beth-meon) is a recurring Moabite town mentioned in Jeremiah 48:23 and Ezekiel 25:9, indicating ongoing Moabite presence in this area despite Reubenite settlement.
18: Jahaz was the site of Sihon's battle against Israel (Numbers 21:23; Deuteronomy 2:32) — the definitive engagement that established Israelite control of the Transjordanian plateau. The city later became a Levitical city (Joshua 21:36). Kedemoth is identified with Salhiya in Jordan. Mephaath appears in Jeremiah 48:21 as a Moabite city, again illustrating the contested nature of this territory across generations.
19: Kiriathaim (Qaryatein) appears in both pre-Israelite and post-conquest lists — it was originally Emim territory (Genesis 14:5) and appears as a Moabite town in Jeremiah 48:1, 23 and Ezekiel 25:9. Sibmah was famous for its vineyards (Isaiah 16:8-9; Jeremiah 48:32). Zereth-shahar on the hill above the valley is unidentified. The inclusion of these specific small towns shows that the allotment process involved detailed local knowledge of the Transjordanian landscape.
20: Beth-peor was directly across from Moses's burial site (Deuteronomy 3:29; Joshua 4:46; Joshua 34:6) and near the valley of Moab where Israel camped before crossing into Canaan. It was associated with the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25). Pisgah is the ridge from which Moses saw the Promised Land (Deuteronomy 3:27; Joshua 34:1). Beth-jeshimoth (near the Dead Sea's northeast shore) marked the boundary with Moab. The Reubenite territory thus encompasses the final wilderness campsites — the transition zone between wilderness and the land.
21: The five Midianite princes — Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba — were killed in the campaign of Numbers 31, the same campaign that killed Balaam. They are described here as "princes of Sihon" — their relationship to Sihon's kingdom shows how the Midianite leadership was intertwined with the Amorite political structure in the region. The campaign against Midian (Num 31) and the defeat of Sihon (Num 21) are thus historically and politically connected.
22: Balaam son of Beor, "the diviner" (haqqosem), was killed by the Israelites in this campaign. The use of qosem (diviner, one who practices qesem — divination by lots or arrows) rather than navi (prophet) is evaluative: despite speaking genuine divine oracles (Num 22-24), Balaam is remembered as a professional diviner who ultimately used his knowledge for personal gain (the Balaam episodes end with his complicity in the Baal Peor apostasy, Numbers 31:16). His death in the conquest is the closure of a narrative that began in Num 22.
23: The Jordan forms the western boundary of Reuben's territory — the river that separates the Promised Land from the Transjordanian possession. The closing formula "this was the inheritance of the Reubenites according to their clans" (lemishpechotam) marks the administrative closure of one tribal allotment. The word nachala (inheritance) carries both legal and theological weight: it is a permanent family possession granted by divine authority, not merely a military acquisition.
24: Gad's territory in Gilead occupied the central Transjordanian highlands — the forested plateau between the Yarmuk and Jabbok rivers. Gilead's forests and pastures made it suitable for the "large amounts of livestock" that motivated the Reubenite and Gadite request for Transjordanian land (Numbers 32:1-5). The region's relative isolation from the tribal heartland contributed to tensions between Transjordanian and Cisjordanian Israel in later periods (cf. the Jephthah-Ephraim conflict, Judges 12:1-6; and the Jabesh-Gilead episodes, Judg 21; 1 Samuel 31:11-13).
25: Jazer (possibly Khirbet Jazzir) and the Ammonite territory mark Gad's eastern boundary. The formula "half the land of the Ammonites" reflects a boundary settlement that acknowledged Ammonite claims east of Gad's territory. Aroer east of Rabbah (Ammon, modern Amman) is different from the Aroer on the Arnon — the territory contains multiple places with similar names, a common feature of Semitic toponymy where descriptive terms (like Aroer = "bare/stripped") were applied to similar landscapes in different regions.
26: Ramath-mizpah (possibly Tell er-Rameh in Gilead) was a strategic highland site — mitspa (watchtower/lookout) reflects its defensive position. The Jabbok River (Wadi Zarqa) was where Jacob wrestled the angel (Genesis 32:22-32), making the Gadite territory historically resonant for Israel's ancestral traditions. Mahanaim (two camps) was where Jacob met the angels (Genesis 32:1-2) and later served as David's refuge from Absalom (2 Samuel 17:24, 27) — the landscape of the allotment carries patriarchal memory.
27: The valley (emek) cities of Beth-haram, Succoth, and Zaphon were in the Jordan Valley lowland, distinct from the Gilead highlands. Succoth is the first campsite after Jacob's Jabbok crossing (Genesis 33:17) — Jacob built booths (sukkot) there, giving the place its name. The Jordan Valley portion of Gad's inheritance thus includes the land of Jacob's own return journey — the patriarchal geography is being reclaimed by Jacob's descendants.
28: The closing formula for Gad repeats the administrative pattern of Joshua 13:23. The emphasis on "towns and their surrounding villages" (arim uvaroteihem) reflects the ANE settlement pattern of a fortified central city surrounded by dependent agricultural villages — the city-state model translated into tribal territory. Each inheritance includes both the urban centers and the agricultural hinterland that sustained them.
29: The half-tribe of Manasseh east of the Jordan received Bashan — the northern plateau extending from the Yarmuk to Mount Hermon. The half-tribe arrangement (with the other half receiving Cisjordanian territory in Josh 17) created a geographically divided tribe, which raised questions about tribal solidarity. Manasseh was Joseph's firstborn son (Gen 48), and the two half-tribe territories together constituted the largest single tribal inheritance.
30: Mahanaim is the northern boundary of Gad's territory and the southern boundary of Manasseh's — the two Josephite half-tribes shared a border in Transjordan. "All of Bashan" including the sixty towns of Jair (the Havvoth-jair, settlements established by Jair son of Manasseh, Numbers 32:41; Deuteronomy 3:14) reflects the earlier Israelite settlement of Bashan during Moses's campaigns. The sixty towns figure appears also in 1 Kings 4:13 in the Solomonic administrative districts, suggesting continuity of this territorial designation into the monarchy.
31: The half of Gilead shared between the two Manassite half-tribes reflects a geographic division that distinguished Highland Gilead (forested, fertile) from the Bashan plateau. The descendants of Makir son of Manasseh (the primary Manassitesubgrouping in Numbers 26:29; Joshua 27:1) received the royal cities of Og. Makir appears earlier as a military leader in Numbers 32:39-40, taking Gilead in the initial Mosaic campaigns.
32: "The plains of Moab across the Jordan from Jericho" was Israel's final wilderness camp — the site of Moses's farewell speeches (Deuteronomy), the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25), and the Transjordanian allotments (Num 32). The closing formula for the Mosaic distribution connects it geographically to the Deuteronomic setting, tying Joshua's administrative work back to its Mosaic foundation.
33: The repetition of Levi's non-inheritance ("the LORD God of Israel is their inheritance, as he said to them") closes the Transjordanian section with the theological key to the entire allotment system. YHWH is Israel's ultimate landlord; he distributes the land according to his own purposes. Levi alone lives by direct divine provision — their "inheritance" is YHWH himself — making them a paradigm of what the whole nation is called to: to find its security in the divine presence rather than territorial possession.
